Why Choosing the Best Dog Toys Matters

Choosing the right dog toy isn’t just about fun — it’s about safety, enrichment, and supporting your dog’s natural instincts. The right toy can reduce boredom, prevent destructive behaviour, and keep your dog physically and mentally healthy.

Whether your dog loves to chew, chase, tug, or solve puzzles, this guide will help you find the best dog toys that are safe, durable, and perfect for their personality.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best dog toys and shop by toy type to find the perfect match for your furry friend.

Toys also play a vital role in strengthening the emotional bond between you and your dog. Shared activities like tug-of-war, fetch, and hide-and-seek with treat-dispensing puzzles build trust and reinforce positive associations. A dog that associates you with fun, rewarding playtime is more likely to respond to commands and feel secure in your presence. Understanding which types of dog toys suit your pet’s temperament ensures every play session is both enjoyable and productive.

Regular play helps maintain a healthy weight, strengthens muscles, and supports cardiovascular fitness. For puppies, appropriate chew toys also promote healthy dental development. Dogs that engage in regular play sessions show fewer signs of anxiety, are better socialised, and tend to be more responsive during training.

Our Bestselling Dog Toys – Top 5 This Year

How to Choose the Right Dog Toys

Choosing the right toy depends on your dog’s age, size, chewing strength, and play style. The right toys keep them active, mentally stimulated, and safe during play

🎯 Match the Toy to Your Dog’s Play Style

Different dogs enjoy different types of play, and understanding those preferences is the first step toward choosing the perfect toy. Picking dog toys that match their natural instincts keeps them engaged longer, reduces frustration, and encourages healthy behaviour patterns throughout their life.

  • Chewers: Look for rubber, nylon, or reinforced materials
  • Fetchers: Choose aerodynamic toys like balls or frisbees
  • Puzzle Lovers: Interactive toys challenge their mind and reduce boredom
  • Tug Players: Rope toys and tug toys encourage interactive play
  • Cuddlers: Soft plush toys are ideal for gentle play

❤️‍🩹 Choose Toys Based on Age & Size

Your dog’s age and size affect what toys are safe and enjoyable.

  • Puppies: Soft toys for teething and gentle chewing
  • Adult Dogs: Durable toys that match their energy level
  • Senior Dogs: Plush or slow‑paced toys that are easy on teeth
  • Small Breeds: Lightweight toys they can easily carry
  • Large Breeds: Heavy‑duty toys that won’t break under pressure

🛡️ Safety & Durability Tips

Always inspect toys for damage and replace them when they show signs of wear. Avoid small parts that can break off and become choking hazards. Pay special attention to stitching on plush toys, and check rubber toys for deep bite marks or cracks that could release small fragments during aggressive chewing sessions.

  • Avoid toys with detachable small pieces
  • Replace frayed or damaged toys immediately
  • Choose non‑toxic, pet‑safe materials
  • Supervise play with rope or plush toys
  • Prefer natural rubber, cotton rope, or food-grade silicone for the safest play
  • Pick the right size toy to prevent choking

🔄 Rotate Toys to Keep Play Exciting

Dogs get bored when they see the same toys every day. Rotating their toys keeps playtime fresh, encourages curiosity, and helps each toy last longer. A simple rotation schedule keeps your dog mentally stimulated and excited to rediscover old favourites.

  • Rotate 3–5 toys weekly to keep playtime fresh and mentally stimulating This also helps extend the life of each toy
  • Store unused toys out of sight to renew interest
  • Mix different play styles — chew, fetch, tug, puzzle
  • Inspect toys before reintroducing them to ensure safety
  • Keep 4–6 active toys available at any time, with others stored away for rotation

Who This Page Is For

This guide is for dog owners who want toys that truly match their dog’s personality and play style. Whether you’re shopping for a power chewer, a gentle cuddler, or a high‑energy fetcher, you’ll find safe, durable options here.

🦴 For Owners of Dog Chewers

Perfect for dogs who love to gnaw and need tough, long‑lasting toys that protect teeth and reduce boredom.

💡 For Dogs Who Are Thinkers

Ideal for curious dogs who enjoy puzzles and interactive play that challenges their mind and keeps them focused.

🎾 For Dogs that Love to Play

Made for energetic dogs who thrive on fetch, playing tug, and active bonding time with their owners.

Dog Toys FAQ

How many toys should my dog have?

Most dogs benefit from 3–5 toys that cover different play styles: chew, fetch, tug, and mental stimulation.

Are squeaky toys safe?

Yes — as long as you supervise play and remove the toy if your dog tries to swallow the squeaker.

What toys are best for aggressive chewers?

Look for rubber, nylon, or reinforced toys labelled “power chewer” or “indestructible.”

How often should I replace dog toys?

Replace toys when they show signs of tearing, loose stuffing, or sharp edges.

Are puzzle toys good for dogs?

Absolutely — they reduce boredom, build confidence, and provide mental stimulation.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Beyond choosing the right dog toys, these practical tips help you get the most from every play session and keep your pet thriving. Whether you are a first-time puppy owner or a seasoned dog parent, there is always something new to learn about enriching your pet’s daily routine through smarter play.

Mental Enrichment Beyond Traditional Playtime

Scent work is one of the most mentally tiring activities for dogs. Hide treats around the house or garden and let your dog use their incredible sense of smell to find them. Training sessions also serve as mental enrichment — teaching new tricks, practising obedience, or learning agility skills all engage your pet’s brain. Social play with other compatible dogs provides both physical exercise and mental stimulation that solo activities simply cannot match.

Getting the Most from Your Dog Toys Budget

Building an effective collection does not require spending a fortune. Focus on variety rather than quantity — a single high-quality tug rope, one durable chew toy, a fetch ball, and an interactive puzzle feeder cover the four main play categories. Investing in well-made dog toys from reputable brands means each item lasts longer and provides safer play sessions than a pile of cheap alternatives.

  • Rotate weekly — keep 3–4 toys in active use and store the rest; swapping makes reintroduced toys feel brand new
  • DIY alternatives — a knotted old towel becomes a tug toy, a tennis ball inside a sock creates a fling-and-fetch object, and frozen treats in a hollow toy provide cooling enrichment on hot days
  • Shop seasonal sales — end-of-season reductions, holiday clearance, and multipacks on subscription services all bring per-item cost down significantly
  • Observe play preferences — a few minutes watching how your dog naturally plays reveals whether fetch items, tug ropes, or puzzles will deliver the most enjoyment
  • Supervise and inspect — always watch the first few sessions with a new toy and remove any product that starts shedding pieces or shows deep bite marks

Explore our curated range at Pet Hub Online, where every product has been selected for quality, safety, and value — from interactive puzzles to durable chew options, we stock everything your companion needs for active, enriching play sessions.

Cleaning and Maintaining Dog Toys

Keeping your dog’s toys clean is just as important as choosing the right ones. Dirty toys harbour bacteria that can lead to stomach upsets and skin irritation. Rubber and silicone toys can be washed with warm soapy water or placed on the top rack of the dishwasher. Plush toys are usually machine-washable on a gentle cycle — always check the label first. Rope toys should be soaked in a vinegar and water solution, rinsed thoroughly, and air-dried to prevent mould growth. Aim to clean high-use toys at least once a week and inspect every toy during cleaning for cracks, tears, or loose parts that could pose a choking risk.

Signs Your Dog Needs New Toys

Dogs communicate their needs through behaviour, and several signs indicate it is time to refresh their toy collection. If your dog starts chewing furniture, shoes, or household items, they may be bored with their current selection. Excessive barking or restlessness during the day can also signal a lack of mental stimulation. Watch for toys that have become too worn to be engaging — a squeaker that no longer squeaks or a ball that has lost its bounce will not hold your dog’s interest. Introducing new textures, shapes, and play challenges helps redirect unwanted behaviour and keeps your dog mentally sharp and physically active throughout every stage of life.

Creating a Play-Friendly Home Environment

Setting up a dedicated play area encourages your dog to engage with their toys independently. Designate a corner of the living room or a section of the garden where toys are always accessible. Use a low basket or open bin so your dog can browse and select what they want to play with. This approach builds independence and gives your pet a sense of ownership over their play space. For indoor play, choose an area with good flooring — tiles or short-pile rugs work best, as they are easy to clean and provide enough grip for active play sessions without risking injury.

Why the Right Dog Bed Matters

Choosing the right dog bed isn’t just about comfort — it’s about support, joint health, and helping your dog get restful sleep. The right bed can reduce pressure on joints, improve overall comfort, and keep your dog cosy throughout the day.

Whether your dog likes to curl up, stretch out, or sprawl across the floor, this guide will help you find dog beds that are safe, durable, and perfectly suited to their size, age, and sleeping style.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best dog beds and shop by bed type to find the ideal match for your furry friend.

Top 5 Best Dog Beds This Year

These are our top picks for comfortable, durable, and safe dog beds for all sizes and breeds.

Rectangle Washable Anti‑Slip Dog Bed →

A soft, supportive everyday dog bed with anti‑slip backing and a fully washable design.

  • Ultra‑soft plush surface keeps dogs warm and comfortable year‑round
  • Anti‑slip bottom prevents sliding on wood, tile, or laminate floors
  • Machine‑washable for easy cleaning and long‑lasting freshness

  • Great for small to medium dogs who love a cosy, cushioned sleep

  • Non‑slip bottom prevents movement on hard floors

  • Off‑the‑ground design improves airflow and helps regulate body temperature
  • Tight, durable mesh fabric supports your dog’s weight without sagging

  • Easy to clean — wipe down or hose off for quick maintenance
  • Ideal for warm rooms, gardens, and summer use

Outdoor Beds →

How to Choose the Right Dog Bed

Choosing the right dog bed comes down to your dog’s age, size, sleeping style, and any joint or health issues. A well‑chosen bed can improve their sleep quality, protect their joints, and keep them comfortable all year round.

🛏️ Match the Bed to Your Dog’s Sleeping Style

Some dogs curl up tightly, others sprawl out, and some like to lean against raised edges. Matching the bed shape to how your dog naturally sleeps makes a big difference in how much they actually use it.

  • Curled‑up sleepers: Donut or bolstered beds give them a cosy, enclosed feeling.
  • Stretched‑out sleepers: Rectangular memory foam beds offer plenty of room to lie flat.
  • Warm sleepers: Elevated cooling beds allow airflow and prevent overheating.
  • Cold sleepers: Plush or faux‑fur calming beds help retain warmth and comfort.
  • Seasonal switchers: Many owners find that having two different dog beds — one for summer and one for winter — works best for year‑round comfort.

❤️‍🩹 Choose Beds Based on Age & Health

Puppies, adults, and senior dogs all have different needs. Older dogs and large breeds, in particular, benefit from extra support that reduces pressure on joints and hips.

  • Puppies: Durable, washable beds that can handle accidents and chewing.
  • Adult dogs: Supportive memory foam or high‑quality filled beds for daily comfort.
  • Senior dogs: Orthopedic beds that ease pressure on joints and make it easier to get up.
  • Large breeds: Thick, high‑density foam that won’t flatten under their weight.
  • Anxious dogs: Bolster‑style beds with raised sides provide a sense of security and help reduce stress.

🛡️ Safety & Durability Tips

A dog bed should be comfortable, but it also needs to be safe and long‑lasting. Paying attention to materials and construction helps you avoid beds that fall apart quickly or pose a risk.

  • Choose non‑toxic, pet‑safe materials.
  • Look for removable, machine‑washable covers.
  • Avoid beds with loose zippers, buttons, or stuffing that can be chewed off.
  • Pick chew‑resistant fabrics for destructive or teething dogs.

📏 Measure Your Dog Correctly

Getting the right size is one of the most important steps. A bed that’s too small will be ignored, and one that’s too big may not feel secure.

  • Measure your dog from the tip of the nose to the base of the tail while they’re lying in their natural sleeping position.
  • Add 20–30 cm to that measurement to ensure they have enough room to stretch comfortably.
  • For curled‑up sleepers, measure them curled into a ball and choose a bed with raised sides for support.
  • Check your dog’s weight as well — heavier dogs need thicker, high‑density foam that won’t flatten.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right dog bed isn’t one‑size‑fits‑all. This guide is designed to help every dog owner find the perfect fit based on their dog’s needs, habits, and sleeping style.

🦴 Senior/Arthritic Dogs

If your dog needs extra joint support or struggles with stiffness, this guide highlights the best orthopedic beds for pain relief and comfort.

🐾 Puppies & Chewers Owners

For dogs who chew, dig, or get messy, Pet Hub Online covers durable, easy‑clean beds that can handle rough play and everyday wear.

🐕 Large Breeds/Heavy Sleepers

If your dog needs more space or tends to flatten cheap beds, we show you the best options for support, size, and long‑lasting comfort.

Dog Beds FAQ

What size dog bed should I buy?

Choose a bed that’s at least 20–30 cm longer than your dog when fully stretched out.

Are orthopedic beds worth it?

Yes — they provide joint support, reduce pressure points, and are ideal for seniors and large breeds.

How often should I replace a dog bed?

Most beds last 1–2 years, depending on use and material quality.

Are donut beds good for anxious dogs?

Yes — the raised edges create a sense of security and help reduce anxiety.

Can dog beds be washed?

Most quality beds have removable, machine‑washable covers. Always check the label.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Beyond choosing the right bed, these practical tips will help you get the most out of your purchase and keep your dog happy and well‑rested.

  • Introduce the bed gradually — place a familiar blanket or toy on it so your dog associates the new bed with comfort.
  • Rotate between two beds to extend the lifespan of each and give your dog variety across seasons.
  • Watch for signs of discomfort — if your dog avoids the bed or seems restless, the size, firmness, or location may need adjusting.
  • Keep a spare cover so you always have a clean one ready while the other is being washed.
  • Place the bed in a consistent location — dogs are creatures of habit and settle faster when their sleeping spot stays the same.
  • Consider your flooring — anti‑slip bases are essential on hardwood and tile, while raised beds work well on cold surfaces.

Why Pet Owners Trust Pet Hub Online

🛡️

How to Clean and Maintain Your Dog Beds

Keeping your dog beds clean is essential for your dog’s health and your home’s hygiene. Most quality dog beds come with removable, machine-washable covers — look for this feature when buying dog beds, as it makes maintenance much easier.

Wash your dog beds at least once a fortnight, or weekly if your dog spends a lot of time outdoors. Use a pet-safe detergent and avoid fabric softeners, which can irritate sensitive skin. Between washes, vacuum your dog beds regularly to remove hair, dander, and dirt.

Inspect your dog beds monthly for signs of wear. Flat spots, lumpy filling, or worn-through fabric means it’s time for new dog beds. Most quality dog beds last 1-3 years depending on use. Rotating between two dog beds can extend their lifespan significantly.

5 Common Mistakes When Buying Dog Beds

The biggest mistake people make with dog beds is choosing the wrong size. Dog beds that are too small leave your dog uncomfortable and unsupported, while oversized dog beds can make smaller dogs feel insecure. Always measure before buying dog beds.

Another common error is prioritising style over comfort when choosing dog beds. While it’s nice to have dog beds that match your decor, your dog’s comfort should always come first. Also, don’t place dog beds in high-traffic areas or near draughts — dogs need a quiet, warm spot to rest.

Seasonal Bedding Tips for Year-Round Comfort

Your pup’s sleeping needs change with the seasons. During summer months, cooling mats, elevated cots, and breathable mesh options help prevent overheating. Look for moisture-wicking fabrics and ventilated designs that promote airflow around your sleeping pup.

Winter calls for insulated, plush options with thermal linings or self-warming materials. Placing the sleeping area away from draughty windows and cold floors makes a significant difference to your pet’s comfort during colder months.

Regardless of season, the sleeping surface should always be appropriate for your pet’s weight and sleeping position. Side sleepers need wider, flatter surfaces, while dogs that curl up prefer bolstered or nest-style options with supportive sides.

Waterproof liners are a worthwhile investment year-round. They protect the filling from accidents, drool, and damp fur after rainy walks. Most quality liners are machine-washable and add an extra layer of comfort while extending the life of your pet’s sleeping area.

Multi-pet households should provide each animal with their own dedicated resting space. Sharing can lead to resource guarding and stress. Position sleeping areas in separate quiet spots where each pet feels secure and undisturbed.

Placement Tips for Your Pet’s Sleeping Area

Where you position your pet’s resting spot matters as much as the product itself. Choose a quiet corner away from high-traffic doorways, draughts, and direct sunlight. A consistent location helps your companion associate that area with rest, making settling down at night or during the day far easier. If your home has multiple levels, consider placing a sleeping option on each floor so your pet always has a comfortable retreat nearby.

Temperature regulation plays a role in placement. During winter, move the sleeping area away from external walls and windows that radiate cold. In summer, tile or wood floors stay cooler than carpeted rooms, making them ideal locations. Elevated options with mesh bases allow air circulation underneath, which is particularly welcome for thick-coated breeds during warmer months.

For households with multiple pets, each animal should have their own designated resting space. Sharing can lead to resource guarding and disturbed sleep. Place sleeping areas within sight of each other if your pets are bonded, but far enough apart that each animal feels they have personal territory. This arrangement promotes harmony and ensures every companion gets the quality rest they need from their dog beds.

Matching the resting surface to your pet’s sleeping style improves comfort dramatically. Dogs who curl up prefer bolstered or nest-shaped options that cradle their body. Pets who sprawl flat need a wide, unconstrained mattress. Side sleepers benefit from a low bolster on one edge that supports the neck without restricting movement. Observing how your companion naturally settles reveals which design will deliver the most restful and restorative sleep for their individual preference.

Why the Right Dog Collar & Leash Matters

Choosing the right dog collar and leash isn’t just about style — it’s about safety, control, and comfort for your dog. The right set can make walks more enjoyable, prevent accidents, and ensure your dog stays secure while out and about.

Whether your dog is energetic, calm, small, or large, this guide will help you find dog collars and leashes that are durable, adjustable, and perfectly suited to their size, temperament, and daily activity. We have made it easier for you to Shop by type.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best dog collars and leashes and shop by style or material to find the perfect match for your furry friend.

Top 5 Best Dog Collars & Leashes

These are our top picks for durable, safe, and stylish collars and leashes for all dog sizes and breeds.

Reflective Dog Collar – Padded Breathable →

View on Amazon →

View on Amazon →

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

From proper fitting to material selection and seasonal care, these practical tips will help you get the most out of your dog’s collar and leash setup.

Proper Fitting Guide

Measuring your pet’s neck circumference before purchasing dog collars online prevents the hassle of returns. Use a soft tape measure positioned where the collar naturally sits, midway between the ears and shoulders, and add two centimetres for comfort. Most manufacturers provide detailed size guides with their products, so matching your measurement to the correct range ensures a snug and safe fit from the first wear.

Lead length affects walking control and your pet’s experience. A standard 1.2-metre lead suits pavement walking and urban environments where close control matters. Double-ended leads that clip to both the front and back of a harness offer maximum steering for strong pullers without the discomfort of collars alone bearing the strain.

Materials and Replacement

Nylon remains the most popular material for dog collars because it is lightweight, washable, and available in countless colours. Leather collars develop character with age and suit owners who prefer a classic appearance, though they require occasional conditioning to prevent cracking. Biothane, a waterproof coated webbing, has surged in popularity among active owners because it resists mud, sand, and water without absorbing odours.

Replace dog collars as soon as you notice fraying, faded stitching, or a weakening buckle. Hardware failure during a walk poses a serious escape risk, especially near roads. Metal D-rings and clasps should move freely without sticking or showing rust. A quick weekly check during your grooming routine catches deterioration early and gives you time to order replacements before the current one fails.

Visibility and Night Safety

Reflective and LED dog collars add a crucial layer of visibility for evening and early-morning walks. Reflective stitching bounces light from car headlamps back to the driver, while USB-rechargeable LED strips provide active illumination visible from over 500 metres. Combining reflective collars with a matching lead creates a complete high-visibility setup that keeps both you and your companion safe during low-light outings.

Personalisation and Seasonal Care

Personalisation options let you combine safety with style. Embroidered name and number directly on the fabric eliminates the risk of tags falling off during rough play. Engraved slide-on plates offer a slimline alternative that sits flat against the neck. Choosing bright colours or unique patterns also makes your pet instantly recognisable at the park, adding a practical identification benefit alongside the aesthetic appeal.

Seasonal adjustments ensure year-round comfort. A lighter, breathable webbing collar suits summer outings, while a padded neoprene-lined option prevents chafing under thick winter coats. Waterproof finishes are ideal for pets who love swimming or walking in the rain, drying quickly and resisting the odour buildup that plagues fabric alternatives. Adapting your selection to the conditions keeps your companion comfortable across every season.

When to Upgrade Your Walking Setup

Even the best dog collars and leads have a limited lifespan. If your dog has outgrown their current collar, if the buckle no longer clicks securely, or if the stitching has started to fray, it is time to invest in a replacement. Puppies in particular grow rapidly and may need a collar adjustment every few weeks during their first year. Keeping a spare collar on hand ensures you are never caught off guard when the old one wears out.

Consider upgrading when your walking routine changes too. A new puppy joining the household, a move to a busier neighbourhood, or a switch from city pavements to country trails all call for different gear. Double-clip leads, hands-free waist belts, and multi-dog couplers can transform your walking experience once you identify your specific needs. Reviewing your setup every six months keeps it aligned with your dog’s behaviour and your lifestyle.

Browse our full selection of leads, harnesses, and accessories at Pet Hub Online to find the perfect match for your companion’s size, breed, and lifestyle.

Who This Page Is For

Whether you’re training, walking, or exploring, each collar and lead here is made to keep your dog secure and stylish. Every product is tested for durability, fit, and ease of use — so you can focus on enjoying time together.

🐾 Everyday Walkers

Perfect for daily strolls and quick outings. These collars and leads combine comfort with reliability, giving you peace of mind every time you clip in.

🎯 Trainers & Active Owners

For those focused on control and technique. Our training collars and long lines help reinforce good habits while keeping your dog safe and responsive.

🌿 Adventurers & Explorers

Ideal for hikes, travel, and outdoor play. Lightweight yet strong gear designed to handle mud, rain, and miles of adventure without compromise.

Dog Collars & Leashes FAQ

Are collars or harnesses better for daily walks?

Both work well — collars are simple and lightweight, while harnesses offer more control for pullers.

How tight should a dog collar be?

Use the two‑finger rule: snug enough to stay secure, loose enough to fit two fingers underneath.

What lead length is best for training?

Short leads (1–1.2m) for heel work; long lines (5–10m) for recall training.

Are retractable leads safe?

They’re great for open spaces but require awareness — always lock the lead near roads or crowds.

How often should I replace a collar or lead?

Check regularly for fraying, rust, or weakened stitching and replace at the first signs of wear.

How to Choose the Right Collar & Leash

Choosing the right collar and leash isn’t just about looks — it’s about comfort, control, and safety. The right setup helps your dog walk confidently while giving you reliable handling in every situation. Here’s how to find the perfect match for your dog’s size, habits, and lifestyle.

🐾 Match the Collar & Leash to Lifestyle – Everyday Walks

Most dogs just need something comfortable, reliable, and easy to clip on. These options are perfect for daily strolls and relaxed outings.

  • Soft, durable nylon or padded collars
  • Standard leads for balanced control
  • Quick‑release buckles for convenience
  • Works for most breeds and sizes

Finding the perfect collar starts with proper sizing. Measure your dog’s neck and add two finger-widths of space — collars should be snug but not tight. For growing puppies, adjustable collars are ideal since you can resize them as your pup grows. Different breeds suit different styles: flat buckle collars work well for most breeds, while martingale collars are better for dogs with narrow heads like Greyhounds.

Material matters when choosing collars. Nylon collars are lightweight, affordable, and come in countless colours. Leather collars are more durable and develop a beautiful patina over time. For water-loving breeds, waterproof collars made from BioThane or coated nylon resist odours and dry quickly.

🎯 For Training & Behaviour Work

For dogs learning manners or needing more structure, training‑focused gear gives you better communication and control.

  • Adjustable training collars
  • Long lines for recall practice
  • Reinforced stitching for safety
  • Helps build consistent habits

Martingale options provide gentle correction without the risk of choking. They tighten slightly when your pet pulls but have a limited closure that prevents over-tightening — ideal for breeds with narrow heads. Head halters like the Halti or Gentle Leader fit around the muzzle and behind the ears, giving exceptional control for strong pullers by guiding the head rather than pulling against the neck.

Many trainers recommend using both a harness and a traditional collar together for maximum safety. Front-clip harness designs redirect pulling, while back-clip versions are comfortable for well-behaved walkers. GPS and smart collars are the latest innovation, combining traditional designs with tracking technology so you can monitor your pet’s location in real-time through a smartphone app.

🌦️ Outdoor Adventures

If your dog loves exploring, choose gear that can handle mud, rain, and rough terrain without wearing out.

  • Weather‑resistant materials
  • Rust‑proof metal hardware
  • Lightweight but strong leads
  • Easy‑grip handles for stability

The standard 1.2-metre fixed lead is the most versatile choice for everyday walks, providing enough freedom for comfortable walking while maintaining good control in busy areas. Look for a comfortable, padded handle to prevent friction burns on your hands. Training leads of 5 to 10 metres give freedom during recall practice in parks and fields.

Retractable leads offer more freedom in open spaces but should be used with caution — they can cause injuries if they snap back or wrap around legs. Never use them near roads, other animals, or in crowded areas. Reflective and LED-equipped leads are essential for evening and winter walks when visibility is reduced, keeping both you and your pet visible to motorists.

🛡️ Comfort & Safety Needs

Some dogs benefit from extra support, especially pullers, small breeds, or dogs with sensitive necks.

  • Padded harness options
  • No‑pull front‑clip designs
  • Reflective stitching for visibility
  • Even pressure distribution for comfort

Safety should be your top priority with dog collars. Check the fit regularly — weight changes, new coats growing in, or collar stretching can all affect the fit. Loose collars can slip off, while tight collars can cause breathing difficulties and skin irritation. Always include an ID tag: under UK law, dogs must wear collars with an ID tag showing the owner’s name and address when in public.

Remove collars during unsupervised play, especially in homes with multiple dogs — collars can snag on crate bars, fencing, or other collars, creating a strangulation risk. Breakaway collars are a safer option for dogs that wear their collars at all times. The two-finger rule is the gold standard for fitting: you should be able to slide two fingers between the collar and your pet’s neck.

Playtime That Keeps Cats Curious and Content

Cats thrive on movement, stimulation, and discovery. Toys aren’t just accessories — they’re tools for health and happiness. Interactive play helps reduce stress, prevent boredom, and encourage exercise. From chasing feathers to solving treat puzzles, each toy supports your cat’s natural instincts while keeping them mentally sharp and physically fit. Explore our curated selection designed to make every play session rewarding and fun.

Top 5 Best Cat Toys This Year

Interactive Feather Wand Toy

Puzzle & Treat Toys →

Tunnel Toys →

Perfect for hiding, chasing, and exploring — great for active cats.

Ball & Rolling Toys →

Simple, durable toys that keep cats entertained and moving all day.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Whether you are a first-time cat owner or a seasoned feline enthusiast, these practical tips and expert insights will help you create a more enriching, stimulating environment for your furry companion at home.

🏠 Creative Indoor Play Ideas

Keeping your indoor feline entertained does not always require expensive purchases. Simple household items can become engaging playthings when used creatively. Cardboard boxes, paper bags with handles removed for safety, and scrunched-up paper balls are all favourites with most cats. Rotating the items available every few days keeps things interesting and prevents your pet from losing interest.

Consider setting up a dedicated play area with climbing shelves, window perches, and activity centres. Vertical space is just as important as floor space for felines. A tall climbing tree near a window gives your pet hours of entertainment watching birds and squirrels outside. Scheduled play sessions at the same time each day help establish a routine that benefits both you and your cat.

  • Use cardboard boxes and paper bags as free play stations
  • Add climbing shelves and window perches for vertical enrichment
  • Schedule play sessions at dawn and dusk when cats are naturally active
  • Food puzzles and snuffle mats turn mealtime into mental enrichment
  • Slow feeders encourage natural foraging behaviour and reduce boredom

🤖 Solo Play and Independent Entertainment

Solo play options are essential for times when you are away from home. Battery-operated wobble toys, automated laser modules with randomised patterns, and treat-dispensing rollers all provide independent entertainment. Ensuring your feline has access to engaging solo activities reduces separation anxiety and prevents the destructive boredom that leads to scratched furniture and knocked-over ornaments while you are at work or running errands.

Scented options infused with valerian or silver vine appeal to the olfactory senses and attract even the most disinterested feline into a play session. Layering multiple sensory experiences — movement, sound, and scent — creates richer interactions that keep your companion mentally sharp and physically fit throughout the day.

  • Battery-operated wobble toys for unsupervised fun
  • Automated laser modules with randomised patterns
  • Treat-dispensing rollers for reward-based solo play
  • Valerian and silver vine scented toys to attract reluctant players
  • Crinkle tunnels and rattle balls for multi-sensory stimulation

💰 Budget-Friendly Cat Toy Ideas

You do not need to spend a fortune on cat toys to keep your feline happy. Some of the best cat toys are surprisingly affordable. Simple crinkle balls, catnip mice, and feather teasers are inexpensive options that most cats absolutely love. DIY cat toys can also be a great option — a cardboard box, a scrunched-up piece of paper, or a sock filled with catnip can provide hours of entertainment alongside purpose-built toys designed for safety and durability.

When shopping on a budget, focus on versatility. A single feather wand can provide weeks of interactive play sessions, and a treat-dispensing ball serves double duty as both entertainment and slow feeder. Look for multi-packs that offer several toy types in one purchase, giving you variety without the premium price tag.

  • Crinkle balls and catnip mice cost under a pound and deliver hours of fun
  • DIY options like paper bags and cardboard tubes are free and effective
  • Multi-packs offer variety and value in a single purchase
  • Versatile toys like feather wands serve multiple play styles
  • Investing in one quality interactive toy often outlasts several cheap alternatives

🐱 Matching Toys to Your Cat’s Life Stage

Age matters when matching products to your pet. Kittens benefit from lightweight items they can bat and chase as they develop coordination and confidence. Adult felines need more challenging options that engage their hunting skills and keep them physically fit. Senior cats often prefer gentle rolling toys or puzzle feeders that provide stimulation without demanding high-energy pouncing. Adjusting your selection as your companion ages keeps engagement levels high throughout every life stage.

Cats that have access to engaging toys tend to be calmer, sleep better, and show fewer signs of stress. If you are introducing a new cat to your home, having plenty of toys available can help them settle in more quickly and feel secure in their new environment.

  • Kittens: lightweight items for batting, chasing, and coordination building
  • Adults: challenging options that engage hunting skills and maintain agility
  • Seniors: gentle rolling toys and puzzle feeders for low-impact stimulation
  • New cats: plenty of toys help them settle and feel secure faster
  • High-energy cats: interactive toys for jumping, chasing, and pouncing

Looking for more? Browse our full range of cat supplies including beds, grooming tools and feeding accessories. We also have a great selection of dog toys if you have a canine companion too. Check out our essential cat supplies guide for expert tips on what every cat owner needs.

Who This Page Is For

This page is for cat owners who want to bring energy, enrichment, and joy into their cat’s daily routine. Whether you’re raising a playful kitten or keeping an older cat active, these toys are designed to suit every personality and home.

🐱 For Playful Kittens

Young cats need stimulation to develop coordination and confidence. These toys encourage curiosity, gentle chasing, and safe exploration while kittens learn balance and movement through fun, interactive play.

🐈 For Adult Cats

Adult cats thrive when they can express natural instincts. Our toys help maintain agility, reduce stress, and prevent boredom — perfect for cats who love to stalk, pounce, and stay sharp even indoors.

🐾 For Multi‑Cat Homes

Shared play builds harmony and reduces tension between cats. These toys promote teamwork, gentle competition, and social bonding, turning group play into a positive, experience for every feline in the household.

Cat Toys FAQ

What types of toys do cats enjoy most?

Cats love toys that mimic prey — feathers, strings, and small moving objects. These trigger natural hunting instincts and keep them engaged.

How often should I rotate my cat’s toys?

Swap toys weekly to keep playtime fresh and prevent boredom. Rotating toys helps maintain interest and excitement.

Are teaser wands safe for unsupervised play?

Use teaser wands only during interactive sessions to avoid tangling or chewing. Store them safely afterward.

Do puzzle toys really help with mental stimulation?

Yes, puzzle toys challenge your cat’s problem‑solving skills, reduce stress, and promote focus.

How can I encourage a shy cat to play?

Start with gentle movements and soft toys. Gradually introduce interactive play to build confidence and trust.

Why Pet Owners Trust Pet Hub Online

🐾

Expert‑Reviewed Picks

Every toy is selected for safety, durability, and feline enjoyment.

⭐

Quality You Can Trust

Our recommendations are refreshed weekly to include the latest, most trusted cat toys available.

✔️

Pet Owner Approved

All reviews come from real cat owners who share honest feedback based on genuine experience.

How to Choose the Right Cat Toys

Choosing the right toys depends on your cat’s age, personality, and play preferences. Look for safe materials, interactive designs, and options that encourage both physical and mental activity. A balanced mix of solo and interactive toys keeps your cat stimulated and happy.

🧶 Consider Texture and Size

Small cats and kittens prefer plush or felt textures, while adult cats may enjoy tougher fabrics that resist wear. Avoid toys with loose threads or detachable parts that could pose choking hazards.

  • Soft, durable materials for comfort
  • Lightweight for easy movement
  • Avoid small choking hazards
  • Washable fabrics for hygiene
  • Secure stitching and seams
  • Choose natural materials like sisal, untreated wood, and organic cotton for sensitive mouths
  • Look for double-stitched seams on plush items and solid rubber on chew products
  • Replaceable feather attachments extend toy lifespan without buying a full replacement

🪶Encourage Interactive Play

Rotate interactive toys to keep sessions fresh. Cats love variety — alternating between feathers, strings, and bells keeps them engaged. Interactive play also strengthens the bond between you and your cat, reducing stress and boredom-related behaviours like excessive scratching or overeating. Aim for two fifteen-minute sessions daily, ideally at dawn and dusk when cats are naturally most active. End each session with a small treat to mimic the satisfying hunt-catch-eat cycle.

  • Teaser wands for active engagement
  • String toys for gentle chasing
  • Laser pointers for fast movement
  • Feather teasers for instinctive play
  • Wand toys with bells for sound stimulation

🧩 Add Mental Stimulation

Start with simple treat dispensers and gradually introduce more complex puzzles. Cats enjoy the satisfaction of earning rewards, and these toys encourage patience and focus. Regular play with puzzle toys helps prevent obesity, which affects around 60% of indoor cats. Just 15–20 minutes of active play each day makes a significant difference to your cat’s health. Multi-sensory toys that combine movement with sound — such as crinkle tunnels and rattle balls — stimulate hearing alongside vision and touch for richer, more rewarding interactions.

  • Treat dispensers for reward‑based play
  • Rolling puzzles for movement and challenge
  • Hide‑and‑seek boxes for curiosity
  • Food mazes for slow feeding
  • Interactive feeders for daily enrichment

🐾 Rotate and Refresh Regularly

Store unused toys in a box and reintroduce them later — your cat will treat them like new. Items stored away for a fortnight feel brand new when reintroduced, reigniting interest and saving you from constantly buying replacements. You don’t need to spend a fortune either — simple crinkle balls, catnip mice, and feather teasers are affordable favourites. Even DIY options like a cardboard box, scrunched-up paper, or a sock filled with catnip can provide hours of entertainment alongside purpose-built toys.

  • Weekly rotation for novelty
  • Seasonal updates for variety
  • Mix textures and colors
  • Add new sounds or shapes
  • Store extras safely and cleanly
  • Dedicate a basket or drawer to your cat’s toys and rotate three or four into active use each week
  • A modest collection of quality toys delivers months of entertainment with smart rotation

🥣 Why the Right Dog Bowl Matters

Choosing the right bowl helps your dog eat comfortably, safely, and at a healthy pace. From slow feeders that prevent gulping to raised stations that support better posture, the right setup makes every meal easier. A good bowl also keeps your home cleaner and your dog happier at mealtime.

Shop by Type

Reliable, easy‑clean bowls for everyday meals.

Slow Feeders →

Designed to slow down fast eaters and support healthy digestion.

Water Fountains →

Why Pet Owners Trust Pet Hub Online

🛡️

⭐

Safe, Pet‑Friendly Materials

Our recommendations are refreshed weekly to Every product featured is designed with your dog’s wellbeing in mind.

All our reviews come from real dog owners who share their honest valued feedback based on genuine real-life experiences.

🐶 Who This Page Is For

Feeding habits vary from dog to dog — this guide helps you choose the right setup for your pet’s needs.

🐾 Everyday Eaters

Dogs who eat at a normal pace and simply need a bowl that’s reliable, hygienic, and easy to maintain. Standard stainless‑steel or ceramic dog bowls work perfectly for these dogs, offering durability and effortless cleaning. They’re ideal for households that want a no‑fuss feeding setup that lasts.

🐕‍🦺 Fast Eaters

Some dogs inhale their food quickly, which can lead to choking, bloating, or digestive discomfort. Slow‑feeder bowls with maze‑style patterns help pace their eating and encourage healthier habits. These bowls add gentle mental stimulation while keeping mealtime safe and controlled.

🐶 Senior Dogs & Large Breeds

Older dogs and bigger breeds often benefit from raised feeding stations that reduce strain on the neck, shoulders, and joints. Elevated bowls help them eat more comfortably and maintain better posture. They’re especially helpful for dogs with arthritis, mobility issues, or deep chests.

Dog Bowls & Feeding FAQ

How often should I clean my dog’s bowl?

Daily cleaning is recommended to prevent bacteria buildup and keep mealtime hygienic.

Are slow feeders good for all dogs?

They’re ideal for fast eaters but also help with digestion and mental stimulation for most breeds.

Do raised bowls help with digestion?

Raised bowls can improve posture and comfort, especially for large breeds and senior dogs.

What size bowl does my dog need?

Choose a bowl that holds at least one full meal with room to spare to prevent spills.they show signs of tearing, loose stuffing, or sharp edges.

Are stainless steel bowls better than plastic?

Absolutely — they Stainless steel is more durable, easier to clean, and less likely to harbour bacteria.boredom, build confidence, and provide mental stimulation.

How to Choose the Right Dog Bowl

Different dogs have different eating habits, speeds, and comfort needs. Choosing the right Dog bowls help to ensure your dog eats safely, reduces mess, and supports better digestion.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Even with the perfect bowl, small habits make a big difference in your dog’s mealtime experience. These practical tips will help you get the most out of your feeding setup and keep your pet healthy day after day.

  • Size Water Bowls Generously: Choose water bowls that hold at least twice your dog’s daily water needs. A dog needs roughly 50ml per kilogram of body weight each day, but active dogs, nursing mothers, and those eating dry food will drink more.
  • Wash Bowls Daily: Even if your dog licks them spotless, bacteria builds up quickly. Stainless steel bowls can go straight into the dishwasher; ceramic and plastic should be hand‑washed with hot soapy water and rinsed thoroughly.
  • Keep Food and Water Apart: Many dogs prefer their water bowl in a different location from their food. Separating the two encourages more frequent drinking and reduces contamination from food debris.
  • Stick to a Feeding Schedule: Consistent meal times help regulate digestion and make it easier to monitor how much your dog is actually eating. Remove uneaten food after 15–20 minutes to discourage grazing.
  • Rotate Bowls for Multi‑Pet Homes: If you have more than one dog, provide separate bowls and feeding stations. This prevents resource guarding, ensures each pet gets the right portion, and reduces mealtime stress.
  • Monitor Eating Behaviour Changes: Sudden changes in appetite, eating speed, or reluctance to eat from the bowl can signal dental pain, nausea, or other health issues worth discussing with your vet.
  • Upgrade as Your Dog Grows: Puppies outgrow bowls quickly. A bowl that was perfect at three months may be too small by six months. Check that the bowl still holds a full portion comfortably and that the height suits your dog’s current size.

Complete Hydration Guide for Your Pet

Proper hydration is essential for your pet’s health, affecting everything from digestion to temperature regulation. The general guideline is 50ml of water per kilogram of body weight per day, but active pets, nursing mothers, and those eating dry food may need significantly more.

Water fountains encourage drinking by providing a continuous flow of filtered water. Many pets prefer moving water to still — it’s an instinct from their wild ancestors who associated still water with contamination. Fountains with carbon filters also remove impurities and improve taste.

Monitor your pet’s water intake as changes can indicate health issues. Excessive thirst may signal diabetes, kidney problems, or Cushing’s disease. Reduced drinking could indicate pain, nausea, or other underlying conditions. Track consumption by filling to a marked level each morning.

In hot weather or after exercise, always provide fresh, cool (not cold) water. Adding ice cubes can make drinking more appealing and help your pet cool down. For outdoor adventures, carry a collapsible portable option and offer water every 20-30 minutes during activity.

For multi-pet households, provide multiple water stations throughout the home. Some pets are reluctant to share, and having options in different rooms ensures everyone stays adequately hydrated throughout the day.

Cleaning and Maintaining Your Dog Bowls

Clean dog bowls are as important for your pet’s health as the food you put in them. Wash food bowls daily with hot soapy water, just as you would your own plates, to prevent bacterial biofilm from forming on the surface. Water bowls should be rinsed and refilled at least once a day, with a thorough scrub every two to three days. Dishwasher-safe dog bowls simplify this routine and ensure a more hygienic clean than hand washing alone.

Stainless steel dog bowls are the easiest to sanitise because their non-porous surface does not harbour bacteria or retain odours. Ceramic options with a glazed finish offer similar hygiene benefits, though they should be replaced if the glaze chips or cracks. Plastic bowls are the hardest to keep truly clean because scratches in the surface trap food residue and bacteria, which is why veterinary professionals consistently recommend stainless steel or ceramic over plastic alternatives.

Outdoor feeding equipment faces additional challenges from weather and insects. A covered feeding station keeps rain, leaves, and ants out of the food while providing shade that prevents water from warming to unappealing temperatures. Bringing outdoor feeding equipment inside overnight discourages wildlife from visiting and extends the lifespan of the bowls themselves.

Investing in quality feeding equipment is one of the simplest ways to support your pet’s digestive health and daily comfort. A well-chosen setup encourages proper eating posture, slows consumption to a healthy pace, and makes mealtimes a calm, enjoyable part of the daily routine. Combined with regular cleaning and thoughtful placement, the right accessories transform feeding from a basic necessity into an opportunity to enhance your companion’s overall wellbeing.

🥣 Match the Bowl to Your Dog’s Eating Speed

Different dogs eat at different paces, and choosing the right bowl helps prevent gulping, choking, and digestive issues.

  • Slow Feeders: Maze designs help fast eaters slow down
  • Standard Bowls: Best for dogs with a calm, steady eating pace
  • Deep Bowls: Ideal for long‑snouted breeds
  • Shallow Bowls: Perfect for flat‑faced dogs like pugs and bulldogs
  • Portion‑Control Bowls: Help manage weight and prevent overeating
  • Breed‑Specific Fit: Flat‑faced breeds like Bulldogs benefit from shallow, wide bowls, while long‑eared breeds like Spaniels need narrow, deep bowls to keep ears clean
  • Right Sizing: Choose bowls that hold your dog’s full meal portion with room to spare — large breeds may need 2‑3 litre capacity, while small breeds are fine with 500ml

🆙 Choose the Right Height for Comfort

Your dog’s size, age, and posture determine whether a raised or floor‑level bowl is most comfortable.

  • Raised Stations: Reduce neck and joint strain for tall or senior dogs
  • Floor‑Level Bowls: Best for small breeds and puppies
  • Stable Stands: Prevent tipping and sliding during meals
  • Dual‑Bowl Setups: Keep food and water at the same height
  • Adjustable Heights: Grow with your dog or suit multi‑dog homes
  • Bloat‑Prone Breeds: Consult your vet before switching to elevated bowls — some research suggests raised feeding may increase bloat risk in deep‑chested breeds

💧 Pick the Best Material for Safety & Hygiene

The material you choose affects durability, cleanliness, and long‑term health.

  • Stainless Steel: Most hygienic, durable, and easy to clean
  • Ceramic: Heavy, stable, and stylish — great for messy eaters
  • Silicone: Lightweight and collapsible for travel
  • Plastic: Budget‑friendly but should be BPA‑free and scratch‑resistant — avoid where possible as plastic can cause chin acne in some dogs and scratches trap bacteria
  • Non‑Slip Bases: Keep bowls steady and reduce spills

🧼 Reduce Mess With the Right Bowl Design

Some dogs are naturally messy eaters or enthusiastic drinkers, and choosing the right bowl design helps keep floors cleaner and mealtimes easier to manage.

  • Non‑Slip Bases: Prevent bowls from sliding across the floor during enthusiastic eating
  • Splash‑Guard Water Bowls: Reduce spills from sloppy drinkers
  • High‑Wall Bowls: Keep kibble contained for dogs who push food around
  • Mat‑Compatible Designs: Pair well with feeding mats for extra mess control
  • Strategic Placement: Set up bowls in a quiet, low‑traffic area where your dog won’t feel rushed — keep food and water bowls in separate spots for best results

Why the Right Cat Bed Matters

Cats sleep between 12 and 16 hours per day on average, which means your cat will spend more than half their life resting. Providing dedicated, comfortable cat beds isn’t merely a luxury — it’s a fundamental part of responsible cat ownership. A quality bed gives your cat a sense of security, a defined territory within the home, and a surface designed to support their body correctly.

Without proper cat beds, your feline may default to sleeping on furniture, laundry baskets, or even cold floor surfaces, none of which provide the insulation, support, or psychological comfort that purpose-built cat beds deliver.

Whether your cat likes to curl up, stretch out, or hide away in a cosy cave, this guide will help you find cat beds that are safe, comfortable, and perfectly suited to their size, age, and sleeping style.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best cat beds and shop by bed type to find the ideal match for your feline friend.

Top 5 Best Cat Beds This Year

These are our top picks for cosy, comfortable, and durable cat beds for all breeds and ages.

Donut Calming Cat Bed →

  • Raised rim pillows — provide head and neck support
  • Machine washable — easy to clean and maintain
  • Anti-slip base — stays put on all floor types
  • Available in multiple sizes — fits kittens to large cats
View on Amazon →

Cat Cave Bed with Hood →

A fully enclosed cave-style bed that gives shy or nervous cats a cosy, private retreat.

  • Enclosed hood design — creates a secure, den-like space
  • Thick padded base — orthopaedic support for joints
  • Soft plush interior — ideal for cold UK winters
  • Lightweight and portable — move it room to room easily
  • Non-toxic materials — safe for all cats
View on Amazon →

Window Perch Cat Bed →

A suction-cup mounted window perch that gives indoor cats a sunny vantage point.

  • Strong suction cups — supports cats up to 15kg safely
  • Breathable mesh base — keeps cats cool in summer
  • Easy installation — no tools or drilling required
  • Perfect for bird watching — endless entertainment for indoor cats
  • Removable washable cover — hygienic and easy to maintain
View on Amazon →

Orthopaedic Memory Foam Cat Bed →

A supportive memory foam bed designed for senior cats and those with joint issues.

  • Memory foam filling — moulds to your cat’s body shape
  • Reduces joint pressure — ideal for arthritic or older cats
  • Waterproof liner — protects the foam from accidents
  • Removable zip cover — machine washable for easy care
  • Non-slip rubber base — stable on any surface
View on Amazon →

Heated Self-Warming Cat Bed →

A self-warming thermal bed that reflects your cat’s body heat without any electricity.

  • Self-warming thermal technology — no wires or electricity needed
  • Ultra-soft faux fur — cosy and inviting for cats

  • Foldable design — great for travel or crate use
  • Budget-friendly — effective warmth without running costs
View on Amazon →

Shop by Cat Bed Type

Find the perfect cat bed for your feline based on their personality, sleeping style, and comfort needs.

Donut & Bolster Beds →

Best for cats who love to curl up. Round, raised-edge designs create a cosy nest that makes cats feel safe and secure while resting their chin on the padded rim.

Cave & Hooded Beds →

Ideal for shy or nervous cats. Fully enclosed cave-style beds mimic den-like hiding spots cats seek in the wild, trapping body heat for extra warmth in winter.

Flat Mat & Cushion Beds →

Perfect for cats who stretch out. Simple padded surfaces ideal for windowsills, sofas, or cat trees. Affordable, easy to wash, and great for confident cats who prefer open sightlines.

Heated & Self-Warming Beds →

Great for senior cats and cold climates. Self-warming materials reflect body heat without electricity, while electric models offer thermostat-controlled warmth for arthritic cats.

Window Perch & Hammock Beds →

Best for indoor cats who love bird-watching. Suction-cup window perches and suspended hammocks give cats a sun-drenched, elevated vantage point for stimulation and relaxation.

Orthopaedic & Memory Foam Beds →

Essential for cats with arthritis or joint issues. Thick memory foam bases distribute weight evenly, relieving pressure points and providing superior spinal support for older cats.

How to Choose the Right Cat Bed

Choosing the right cat bed comes down to your cat’s age, size, sleeping style, and any joint or health issues. A well-chosen bed can improve their sleep quality, protect their joints, and keep them comfortable all year round.

📏 Size & Fit

Getting the right size is one of the most important steps. A cat bed that’s too small will be ignored, and one that’s too big may not feel cosy enough for cats that prefer snug spaces.

  • Measure your cat from nose to base of tail while lying stretched out, then add 20–25 cm.
  • For donut or cave beds, the interior diameter should match this measurement.
  • Large breeds like Maine Coons need XL beds — always check internal dimensions.
  • In multi-cat homes, provide one bed per cat plus one extra to prevent resource guarding.
  • Kittens need small, enclosed cat beds — they lose body heat quickly and crave security.
  • If your cat changes sleeping position often, choose a bed slightly larger than the stretched-out measurement to accommodate all postures.

🧵 Material & Fabric

The material affects warmth, durability, ease of cleaning, and how attractive the bed is to your cat. Different cats have strong tactile preferences.

  • Fleece & Sherpa: Soft, warm, affordable, and machine washable — a great all-rounder.
  • Plush & Velvet: Premium feel, attractive in modern interiors, irresistible for kneading cats.
  • Linen & Cotton: Breathable and ideal for warmer months or centrally heated homes.
  • Memory Foam: Exceptional joint support for senior cats — with removable washable covers.
  • Seasonal rotation: Many owners keep two beds — a breathable cotton one for summer and a thermal-lined one for winter.
  • Anxious cats: Soft, plush fabrics with raised edges or hoods help nervous cats feel safe and enclosed.

📍 Placement & Location

Even the most perfectly chosen bed will go unused if placed somewhere your cat finds unsuitable. Cats are particular about their sleeping environments.

  • Place beds in warm, quiet corners away from high-traffic areas and draughts.
  • Near radiators or sunny windowsills are consistently popular positions.
  • Many cats prefer elevated spots — on bookshelves, cat trees, or sturdy furniture.
  • In multi-storey homes, place a bed on each floor — especially for senior cats.
  • For shy or anxious cats, place the bed in a low-traffic corner or inside a closet with the door ajar — enclosed, quiet locations help them feel secure.
  • Observe your cat’s natural sleeping spots for a few days — then place the bed in their preferred location for the best chance of adoption.

🧹 Cleaning & Maintenance

Regular cleaning is essential for your cat’s health and the hygiene of your home. Unwashed beds accumulate fur, dander, and bacteria.

  • Choose beds with removable, machine-washable covers for easy maintenance.
  • Wash at 40°C every 2–3 weeks with a pet-safe detergent.
  • Shake outdoors and lint-roll before washing to prevent clogging the machine.
  • Replace beds when padding flattens or loses support — typically every 1–3 years.
  • For senior or arthritic cats, choose orthopaedic beds with waterproof liners — accidents become more common with age, and the liner protects expensive memory foam.
  • Rotating between two beds extends each bed’s lifespan and ensures your cat always has a clean option available.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right cat bed isn’t one-size-fits-all. This guide is designed to help every cat owner find the perfect fit based on their cat’s needs, habits, and sleeping style.

🐱 Senior & Arthritic Cats

If your cat needs extra joint support or struggles with stiffness, this guide highlights the best orthopaedic and memory foam beds for pain relief and comfort.

🐾 Kittens & Young Cats

For new kittens needing a cosy, secure space, Pet Hub Online covers enclosed cave beds, self-warming inserts, and washable options perfect for settling young cats into their new home.

🏠 Indoor Cats

Indoor cats need comfortable beds that provide warmth, stimulation, and a sense of territory. We cover window perches, hammocks, and elevated beds that keep indoor cats happy and rested.

Cat Beds FAQ

Why does my cat ignore their new cat bed?

This is one of the most common frustrations among cat owners. Cats are creatures of habit and strong olfactory preference, and a new cat bed that smells of the factory, packaging, or another animal may simply be unappealing at first. To encourage your cat to use new cat beds, place an item of your worn clothing inside or nearby to introduce a familiar scent. You can also sprinkle a small amount of catnip on or near the bed, or move the bed to a location you’ve observed your cat already gravitating towards. Give it at least two to three weeks before concluding your cat won’t use the bed — patience is key.

How many cat beds does my cat need?

The standard recommendation is one cat bed per cat, plus one extra. For a single-cat household, this means two cat beds — one primary sleeping spot and one alternative so the cat can choose based on their mood. In practice, many cats naturally gravitate towards three or four favourite sleeping spots around the home, so having cat beds available in each of those areas helps redirect them away from your furniture. In multi-cat homes, having more cat beds than cats is strongly advised to prevent resource competition.

Are heated cat beds safe?

When purchased from reputable brands and used correctly, heated cat beds are entirely safe. Self-warming cat beds that use reflective materials rather than electricity carry no electrical risk and are safe to leave out unsupervised. Electric heated cat beds should always bear a recognised UK safety mark and include automatic temperature regulation to prevent overheating. Never use a human heating pad as a substitute for proper heated cat beds, as these are not designed for animals and can cause serious burns. Always provide your cat with the option to move away from a heated bed if they become too warm.

Should I let my cat sleep in my bed or get them their own cat beds?

This is a personal choice, and both approaches are valid. Many cat owners enjoy the companionship of their cat sleeping alongside them, and research suggests this can strengthen the human-animal bond. However, having dedicated cat beds available gives your cat an alternative sleeping option and means you have a fallback if you prefer an undisturbed night. It’s also worth noting that cats are crepuscular and highly active in the early morning hours, which can disrupt human sleep. Providing comfortable cat beds in the bedroom — on a chair or at the foot of the bed — can satisfy your cat’s desire to be near you while giving you the option of boundaries.

How long do cat beds typically last?

The lifespan of cat beds varies considerably by quality and construction. Budget fleece cat beds that are washed frequently can begin to pill, lose shape, or lose their insulating properties within six to twelve months. Mid-range cat beds with quality construction and removable washable covers typically last two to four years. Premium orthopaedic cat beds with high-density foam can last five years or more, though the fabric cover may need replacing sooner. Signs that cat beds need replacing include flattened or lumpy padding that no longer provides support, permanent odours that washing cannot remove, or visible wear such as torn seams or exposed foam.

Can I make my own cat beds?

Absolutely. DIY cat beds are a popular option for crafty owners, and a simple sewn cushion cover filled with polyester stuffing or old fabric scraps can make a perfectly functional resting spot your cat may adore. Repurposed items such as old suitcases lined with a cushion, wooden crates with a mat insert, or cardboard boxes with fleece blankets all make creative and cost-effective cat beds. The main considerations are safety — avoid materials with loose threads, toxic dyes, or parts that could be ingested — and washability, as homemade cat beds still need to be regularly cleaned to stay hygienic.

Why Pet Owners Trust Pet Hub Online

🛡️

How to Clean and Maintain Cat Beds

Keeping your cat beds clean is essential for your cat’s health and your home’s hygiene. Most quality cat beds come with removable, machine-washable covers — look for this feature when buying cat beds, as it makes maintenance much easier.

Wash your cat beds at least once a fortnight, or weekly if you have multiple cats or outdoor cats. Use a pet-safe detergent and avoid fabric softeners, which can irritate sensitive skin. Between washes, vacuum your cat beds regularly to remove hair, dander, and dirt.

Inspect your cat beds monthly for signs of wear. Flat spots, lumpy filling, or worn-through fabric means it’s time for new cat beds. Most quality cat beds last 1–4 years depending on use. Rotating between two cat beds can extend their lifespan significantly.

5 Common Mistakes When Buying Cat Beds

The biggest mistake people make with cat beds is choosing the wrong size. Cat beds that are too small leave your cat cramped and uncomfortable, while oversized cat beds can make cats feel exposed and insecure. Always measure before buying cat beds.

Another common error is prioritising style over comfort when choosing cat beds. While it’s nice to have cat beds that match your décor, your cat’s comfort should always come first. Also, don’t place cat beds in high-traffic areas or near draughts — cats need a quiet, warm spot to rest. Ignoring your cat’s sleeping preferences is another mistake: observe whether they curl up, stretch out, or hide before choosing a bed style.

Seasonal Bedding Tips for Year-Round Comfort

Your cat’s sleeping needs change with the seasons. During summer months, breathable cotton mats, elevated perches, and mesh-based window beds help prevent overheating. Look for moisture-wicking fabrics and ventilated designs that promote airflow around your sleeping cat.

Winter calls for insulated, plush options with thermal linings or self-warming materials. Placing the sleeping area away from draughty windows and cold floors makes a significant difference to your cat’s comfort during colder months.

Regardless of season, the sleeping surface should always be appropriate for your cat’s weight and sleeping position. Side sleepers need wider, flatter surfaces, while cats that curl up prefer donut or nest-style options with supportive raised edges.

Waterproof liners are a worthwhile investment year-round. They protect the filling from accidents and damp fur. Most quality liners are machine-washable and add an extra layer of comfort while extending the life of your cat’s sleeping area.

Multi-cat households should provide each animal with their own dedicated resting space. Sharing can lead to resource guarding and stress. Position sleeping areas in separate quiet spots where each cat feels secure and undisturbed.

Placement Tips for Your Cat’s Sleeping Area

Where you position your cat’s resting spot matters as much as the product itself. Choose a quiet corner away from high-traffic doorways, draughts, and direct sunlight. A consistent location helps your cat associate that area with rest, making settling down at night or during the day far easier. If your home has multiple levels, consider placing a sleeping option on each floor so your cat always has a comfortable retreat nearby.

Temperature regulation plays a role in placement. During winter, move the sleeping area away from external walls and windows that radiate cold. In summer, tile or wood floors stay cooler than carpeted rooms, making them ideal locations. Window perches with mesh bases allow air circulation, which is particularly welcome for thick-coated breeds during warmer months.

For households with multiple cats, each animal should have their own designated resting space. Sharing can lead to resource guarding and disturbed sleep. Place sleeping areas within sight of each other if your cats are bonded, but far enough apart that each animal feels they have personal territory. This arrangement promotes harmony and ensures every companion gets the quality rest they need from their cat beds.

Matching the resting surface to your cat’s sleeping style improves comfort dramatically. Cats who curl up prefer donut or bolstered options that cradle their body. Cats who sprawl flat need a wide, unconstrained cushion. Side sleepers benefit from a low bolster on one edge that supports the neck without restricting movement. Observing how your cat naturally settles reveals which design will deliver the most restful and restorative sleep for their individual preference.

Why Cat Grooming Matters

Many cat owners assume that because cats self-groom, they don’t need any help. In reality, regular grooming is a shared responsibility that goes far beyond keeping your cat’s coat looking good. Proper grooming sessions strengthen the bond between you and your pet, allow you to check for lumps, skin irritation, fleas, and parasites, and help you detect early signs of health conditions before they become serious.

Whether your cat has a short, medium, or long coat, this guide will help you find the best cat grooming tools that are safe, effective, and perfectly suited to their breed, age, and grooming needs.

These are our top picks for essential cat grooming tools that make grooming easier and more enjoyable for both you and your cat.

Self-Cleaning Slicker Brush →

A gentle yet effective slicker brush that removes loose fur, prevents matting, and keeps your cat’s coat smooth and healthy.

  • Self-cleaning button — retract bristles for easy fur removal
  • Fine bent wire pins — gentle on skin, tough on tangles
  • Ergonomic handle — comfortable grip for longer grooming sessions
  • Suitable for all coat types — short, medium, and long hair

  • Suitable for all cat sizes — from kittens to large breeds
  • Includes nail file — smooth rough edges after trimming
View on Amazon →

Cat Grooming Glove →

A soft rubber grooming glove that removes loose fur while mimicking the sensation of being stroked — perfect for nervous cats.

  • 255 silicone tips — gentle massage while removing loose hair
  • Feels like petting — ideal for cats who dislike brushes

  • Works on wet or dry fur — versatile for bath time too
  • Easy to clean — peel fur off the glove in one motion
View on Amazon →

Cat Shampoo (Natural & Gentle) →

A pH-balanced, natural cat shampoo formulated with gentle ingredients that clean without stripping essential oils from your cat’s coat.

  • pH-balanced for cats — won’t irritate sensitive skin
  • Natural ingredients — aloe vera, oatmeal, and chamomile
  • No harsh chemicals — free from parabens, sulphates, and dyes
  • Leaves coat soft and shiny — conditions while it cleans
  • Pleasant natural scent — not overpowering for your cat
View on Amazon →

Cat Dematting Comb →

A professional-grade dematting tool with rounded blade edges that safely cut through mats and tangles without pulling or hurting your cat.

  • Stainless steel blades — durable and rust-resistant
